speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want me to slate?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2 with Kaethe Wells.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ms. Wells, before the night of Kristallnacht was over, were there other Jewish families close by that you witnessed this same thing happening to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It did happen to them. But we did not-- it was pitch dark. And actually, what we did during that night, take broom and shovel and just shovel and shovel and made heaps in the yard so that we could-- you could walk, because you couldn't walk. And as it-- as it started to get light, we saw that-- what was on the street. And my sister and I went out and cleaned the street the best we could. We-- mostly we needed the beds, and blankets, and bed linens. Of course, a lot disappeared during that night. But we really didn't care about that. It was so unimportant. But what was important was, as we were cleaning the street, my sister said, look at that car. A car drove by. A man got out and photographed. And it was from Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we told our parents that, they said, good. So now the foreign countries know. Now they have pictures. They know. I-- I-- because we couldn't understand why we didn't hear from, you know, anybody. But they did know. They-- they did know. OK, then at 10 o'clock, a local policeman came and said, Doctor, put on your heavy coat and come with me. Where to? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother put his-- my father was a highly decorated officer from World War I. And so my mother put the papers to the medals in his pocket. He wore his heaviest coat.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day went on and we heard through a grapevine that the Jewish men and boys from the village were taken to Frankfurt to a hall where they had to assemble. Nothing then for two or three days, when our mailman came by. And he said, your husband is in Buchenwald. What is Buchenwald? Well, Buchenwald is a concentration camp. What is a concentration camp? Well, it's like a camp, like a prison camp. They're really building it. And where is Buchenwald? We looked on the map, and it was in the mountains. And my mother said, oh, good. He's in fresh air and had no idea what it was. Nothing. And then stories were going around. It's like Dachau. Now, we knew what Dachau was. What did you know about Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau we knew was a prison camp where people were treated very, very badly. See, Dachau was the oldest camp. The others all-- so a week went by, and my mother was up again. She-- she was-- she was wonderful. And she said, I'm going to write to Hitler. And we said, oh, don't, don't do that. She said, I have to do something. A man like your father just does not disappear. She-- she said, I know what I'll do. I'll write to Emmy Goring, as woman to woman. She did. She had an answer back within days. And the secretary of Emmy Goring wrote that she is very sorry but she cannot interfere in politics. And if she-- she should write to her husband. My mother wrote to Field Marshal Goring. Never heard from him, never. Now comes my father's story. He was in-- three weeks, we didn't hear anything. Yes, we had the card. We have a card from Buchenwald. And when we read it, we knew immediately my father wrote it. But somebody dictated what he should say, because that was not the way he talked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the card say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The card said, dear, dear wife and children. I am sitting here. I am well. Please do not send letters or packages. I have-- I'm not allowed to receive anything. That was just about the extent of the card. But at least he was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he-- to your knowledge, was he charged with any crime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being Jewish. That was a crime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what they said when they took him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't say anything. I say that. No. His-- our crimes-- we're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone tell you why they were taking him away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any other people being taken away at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't see anybody being taken away. He was-- he went to the police station and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the other Jewish families contact you to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We all sort of stayed by ourselves-- ourselves. It-- it-- it was like we were waiting-- we were full of fear. And we were-- we were waiting. And the three of us slept in one room, actually pushed two beds together. We needed the closeness. And it was very eerie in that huge, destroyed house. Was it-- the whole thing was-- Mother said one day, I think we each ought to pack a suitcase. We don't know what's going to happen. You know, from then on, we always had suitcases packed until my sister took old rugs and made huge knapsacks. From then on, knapsacks were always in the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you keep in the knapsacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes. Mother would say, take winter clothes. Take summer clothes. Take practical things. Nothing-- and take little mementos that you don't want to lose, because anything that is not in there, you'll never see the rest again, which happened, which, you know, was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to put any money, or food, or jewelry in the knapsacks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was your father gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was gone three weeks. He came back after three weeks. It was late at night. I was asleep. My sister was asleep. My sister, after two weeks, took one of the maids' rooms on the third floor and made it to her room. And Mother and I stayed together.

speaker: interviewee
text: I woke up. It was dark. And I heard a sound from downstairs. It sounded like a wounded animal. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Mother came in the room and said, go in the other room and don't open the door. And I said, what's going on? She said, shh, your father is back.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came that night. Mother stripped him in the-- in the yard, in the courtyard totally, then lifted him. The man weighed approximately 156 pounds, normally. [SOB] She picked him up like he was a doll and carried him upstairs, let water run in the tub, put him in the tub, washed him, gave him a bath, put warm pajamas, and put him in bed, and then ran downstairs and spooned soup. And then he said he wants to see the girls. Now she put a robe on him. And he came in the hallway. I didn't recognize him, because they had shaved his head. But he-- his face looked like a skeleton. You've seen a skeleton. And when he embraced us, it was-- you felt nothing but bones. [SOB] I screamed so that Mother had to slap me and put me to bed. And then the next morning, I finally-- this-- this was one of the most horrible experiences of my life, because this was my father and it wasn't my father. He was so sick. Our doctor came at night. Of course, he wasn't allowed to come. And he was a very, very Catholic man. And he said, Doctor, please, tell me. I have a daughter. I have to raise her. Please tell me what happened. And my father said, I cannot tell you, because I was told if I opened my mouth to anybody, not only will I disappear, but my wife and my daughters will disappear. I cannot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when-- I'm going ahead. When we crossed the border from Germany into Yugoslavia, my mother said, Max, tell us. And that's when he told. But what he said, Liesel, what did you do? This was the next day, you know. How come you've been discharged? The other Jewish men-- they came little by little. But not after three weeks. They were gone for weeks. Those who could prove that they were ready to immigrate to America were discharged. So many of them were ready. They didn't have the papers with them. And so it took that much longer for them. And why he was discharged-- he said, Liesel, what did you do? And she said, what? He said, did you write to Goring? And she said, yes. He said, that's what I thought. He said, I was over the loudspeaker. The Jew Max Schohl is to appear at the commandant. When you are called to the commandant, you-- you disappear. You don't go back to the barracks. So he went. And he went in. And this officer sat behind the desk. He did not look up when my father came in. He said, Max Schohl, number so-and-so, what is your connection with Field Marshal Goring? And my father said, oh, my god, Liesel, what did you do? He said, no connections. He said, we have a note here from Field Marshal Goring to have you released and send you home. And Father, of course, didn't open his mouth. And that's when he looked up. And he said, how were you treated? Papa said, fine. Are there any marks on you? No. How was the food? Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you are being released now. You take the train to Frankfurt and then take the train to Florsheim. They gave him-- whatever it is they gave them, I don't know, so that he could take the train. He said, that's when he said, if you ever-- and he was released. And that's how he got home. And, of course, as I said, we had to wait two more years before we found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not going to ask you to skip ahead. But I would like you to tell me now what it was that he told your mother had happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, my mother said to him, Max, didn't you show the papers to show who you are? He said, Liesel, not a chance. They took us from Frankfurt by train. We didn't know where we were going. But it was Buchenwald. And we still didn't know what it-- what it was. And we still didn't know what a concentration camp was, even though we were in it. He said, when we arrived, the SS stood in two rows-- just a small walkway. And each prisoner had to run. And each of the SS men had one of those rubber hoses-- sticks in their hand. And each prisoner had to walk through. Of course, they ran through it, because they were beaten from both sides. That was our welcome to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, and you think I'm going to stay there and said, huh? No way, he-- never. He said, the reason I am still here and still alive is thanks to a very young Catholic priest who preached from the pulpit-- is it pulpit? Yeah-- against the Nazis. He's there for life. He will never get out. And he was my bunk mate-- mate on the bunk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, I don't want to know who you are. You know who I am. I'm a priest. I'll be here for life. You will get out. I want you to get out. My father started talking about his wife and his daughters. He said, I don't want to hear. You are a prisoner here. All you do now is live for today. You don't think about tomorrow. And you don't think about yesterday. You don't have a wife. And you don't have daughters. You only think for today. And my father couldn't eat. And he wouldn't eat. And this priest said, if I have to stuff it down your throat, you are going to eat that stuff they give us, because I want you to be alive. I want you to leave here. And you cannot if you keep thinking in the past. You can't-- you think today I'm going to stay alive. And then tomorrow morning, if you're lucky enough to wake up, you say again, today I will do everything to stay alive. That's-- this Catholic priest-- it's been so many years. And I still pray and thank him. That-- he's there-- he was there for life. But he saved my father's life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did your father stay at home now when he came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first thing he said, Liesel, we're going to leave. Up until then, he didn't want to leave Germany. He kept saying, it will blow over. The German people won't let this happen. This was before the Kristallnacht. It won't-- we don't have to go. All our friends, little by little, have left. And my father kept saying, not-- not to me. Uh-uh. I have nothing but friends. But he saw the friends turned, all but a few. He said, we are leaving Germany if we have to go barefoot. We don't have to take anything. But we are leaving. Now we have to try and get out. And that is when this desperate attempt started to get us out. First, he wrote to his cousins Julius and Norma in Charleston, with whom he had corresponded before Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is Charleston, West Virginia.

speaker: interviewee
text: West Virginia, right. And they used to correspond with each other. His aunt, the mother of Julius and Norma, used to come back to Germany to visit. I don't remember her. But she remembered me when I came to Charleston. So he wrote to them and said, we need affidavits. We need to get out of here. They had-- they did not have to give any money. They had to be able to prove that, if we come, we will not be a burden to the government. Well, Cousin Julius Hess and Norma, at that time, Kaufman, managed to give us-- show that we got the affidavit. With the affidavit, you get a quota number. Our quota number came due in 1944. So, of course, we knew where we would be in '44. So now, that desperate attempt-- we knew there were exceptions made. All of 1939, there were telegrams and letters. I have to go over this kind of quick, because, as I said, this goes on for a year. There were people here. The then governor of West Virginia, the then senator, United States senator. I have the letters to prove this, because my Cousin Julius saved everything, wrote to the State Department, let this man come. He is a chemist. He did not come. This was under President Franklin D. Roosevelt. And what shows from the letters we have, that government at that time was anti-Semitic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we saw that it was impossible to get to America, my father called London. And all London-- he had a friend there named Mr. [? Merzmer. ?] [? Max Merzmer, ?] bless him. He had almost, almost, but not quite, before the war broke out in '39, couldn't get to England. But I'll tell you what kind of a man my father was. [? Merzmer ?] arranged a visitors visa, a two-week's visitors visa for my father to come to London. That was in the summer of '39-- the war started, I believe, in September. Just to get him out of Germany. He knew, once he was there, he's never going to come back. But my father and my mother begged him, because she knew him. Max, don't come back. Don't come back. We will be fine. I have four brothers in the German Army. My father said, I'm coming back. Maybe if my mother had threatened him-- I don't know. You know, you think about these things afterwards. When he got to the German-Dutch border, full of SS, and they looked and they said, ah, another rat is leaving the ship. Good riddance the Jew. My father said, gentlemen, I'm coming back. And they laughed out loud. They said, look at the Jew. He's-- [LAUGHS] he's coming. His friends in London, they showed him the house that they had for us. He had a job as a chemist. I have to go back. If I don't go back, I will never know what's going to happen to my wife and my daughters. They go into a camp. I cannot do this. I have to go back. He went back. When he got to the Dutch-German border, the SS men-- nobody said a word. They turned around and walked away. And my father said, gentlemen, I told you I would come back. They had never met-- you know, the Jews were pictured as liars, cheaters, murderers. Here was a man of honor who only gave his word. And he kept his word.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I think back, oh, why? Things would have turned out so different. Anyhow, he came back. And England closed its doors. And that was-- we children never saw it. But Mother told us afterwards, that's when he tried to commit suicide. He figured his wife and his children will be well off without him. And my mother said, Max, don't do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: She remembered a friend who went to Yugoslavia. He was a journalist. He became very friendly with then King Alexander-- King Peter's father. And he lived-- yeah, the king gave him a house. And he lived, and he was doing very well. Mother wrote to him. His name was Jacob Altmeyer. And this is the situation. He had a sister-in-law still living in Florsheim. The husband and the daughter were already in America. The woman was alone. So Jacob Altmeyer wrote back, bring my sister-in-law and you will get visitors visa. We had, all of us, the five of us then went together. All of us had a three-week visitors visa. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Had-- oh, no, I forgot something. After my father got back from Buchenwald, once a week he had to report to the Gestapo in Frankfurt. And each time, they said, show us what you have done for your immigration. You know what's going to happen. There is a deadline. And, well, my god, we did everything we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- oh, no, I have to go back again. Father had a friend, a Mr. Mueller, who lived in Sao Paulo, Brazil. And he wrote to him. And he said, of course you come. You come. I will see that you get your visa. But the-- the-- the ships-- the voyage has to be paid in American dollars. So you-- we needed $2,000 for the four of us to go from Genoa to San Paolo. This is where our American cousins came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a lot of money in those days, especially if you were not a rich person. Two of them were very rich. But two were not. And finally, each gave $500. And it was sent to Holland-- the Amsterdam, Holland, bank. And our passports went to the Brazilian Consulate in Berlin. It takes time. It takes time. It takes time. There was no time left. So that's then when my mother wrote and said, we're on our way to Brazil. We're just waiting. So we told the Gestapo that we are going temporarily to Yugoslavia and from there to Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what-- what date? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date we left Germany-- it was Easter. No, we arrived in Zagreb Easter Sunday, 1940. We got permission to pack lifts. We had four or five of these huge boxes made where you could put household stuff in. And these boxes were sealed by the Gestapo and sent to Genoa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bedding, pots and pans, rugs that we could save from the glass that-- Mother had taken all the-- the-- the torn and broken pictures, had them mended as much as she could. Of course, the China and the crystal was gone. But books, some books we saved, and clothes, and no furniture, no, of course not, just so that we had something to sleep. We had something to cook in. And-- and sell the Persian rugs. Boy, did we sell the Persian rugs. And, of course, what nobody knew in 1936-- it was either '35, '36, I say that again, there-- a law came out. Every Jewish woman had to bring her jewelry to the Gestapo. She will get a receipt for it. And my mother said, I'm not taking my pearls. My father said, Liesel, you have got to take everything. I will go with you. Let's put it all in a box. And she said, all right, I'll put it in a box. She put things away that were not known and never told my father, because he would not-- not have allowed that. During-- this is how the packing proceeded. We had two packers that came. We didn't hire them. The Gestapo sent two men in uniform, one upstairs, one downstairs, one packer upstairs, one packer downstairs. And he would-- the packer-- we just stood there. We weren't allowed to touch anything. It started early in the morning. He would take a dress. And the Gestapo man would come-- a pair of underpants. That's how it was done. And imagine, of course, nobody knew. But I could not imagine why my mother was so nervous. She was very nervous. And we found out afterwards why.
